item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh…. just a minor question… Since the guys from the future know what happened… why don't they tell the entire story to the Foundation of the present? Like, they came here, they said some cryptic stuff about the rugby team and they left? That… doesn't make much sense, but I liked the article and the result is an upvote. Good job, but I'd like that part clarified.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I sincerely hope that nobody ever writes something explaining the "Australian Church of Australia", and that it remains a weird thing that is only ever referred to in passing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to agree with Petrograd and Levi above. You set up a good hook, but you don't do anything with it. However, I'm a big fan of the idea.

Reluctant +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This. It's a major problem that I've also had with many articles you've written, actually. Great ideas that don't receive the necessary development in order to be great articles. So, similar vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This feels like two gallons of story in a ten gallon bucket.

+ show block
– hide block

The core of it is something I can get behind. Delta-T members get sent back in time to stop a -K scenario (hypothetically, anyway), and the Foundation trusts them and is going to any means to figure out what is up is a neat story. It's got shades of 12 monkeys in a pretty good way.

Unfortunately, that feels like maybe a paragraph or two's worth of content. The rest of the space just feels like an excuse to talk about New Zealand as much as possible in "funny" ways. (*funny because it's random, funny because New Zealand. Not actually funny.) Ending on NZK especially feels like a lame punchline, and this isn't a -J. (Yes, I know mainlisters can be funny, but so much of this feels like lame joke material.)

It's also deliberately a story without an ending, which probably wouldn't be so unsatisfying of the article was shorter.

Also, unless this is an alternate universe from the linked article and Delta-T officially exists, the fact that the Delta-T folks are taken so seriously and treated well it's entirely at odds with their portrayal there in a way that never feels particularly addressed. This article doesn't feel even remotely serous, so adopting plot elements from very serious SCPs without any kind of realignment just feels weird.

No vote, I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the detailed response! I'm actually just over halfway through law school in NZ! I'd like to thank my lecturers for drilling 'There is no singular constitution of New Zealand' into my head. From my perspective, the general population seems pretty neutral on whether we codify our constitution, but there definitely is a movement to codify and entrench it led by Sir Geoffrey Palmer. I do agree with Halitherses that perhaps redacting the last two digits of the date of codification would provide less distracting for others with knowledge of the current state of affairs. That being said, the idea that this is simply set in a different universe satisfies things for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd suggest redacting the last two digits of 2009, to keep the possibility open that it is established in future, as non-anomalous historical contradictions divert reader attention from the intentionally anomalous aspects.

That said, it's really cool to see a skip making use of NZ!

item.title: Re: Are we talking about the All Blacks or the Kiwis?
item.content:encoded: That's it, yeah. From snippets of it in the Constitution it seems like it's written to spec, but they don't actually have it on-record anywhere. So the only real way to figure out what's going to happen is interrogating the 3868-B instances.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Be honest, did you write this just so you could make an NZK class scenario a thing? +1

item.title: Are we talking about the All Blacks or the Kiwis?
item.content:encoded:
Of course, my question isn't really about which rugby team you made a passing reference to ;)

I genuinely can't wrap my head around the sentence "While excerpts of the specification for 73-Waitangi match the official format of other Foundation contingencies, no such contingency exists at the time of writing".

Do you mean that there is no official Foundation record of 73-Waitangi? So they're containing all the -B instances to try to work out what's going to happen in the future?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm honoured someone actually bothered to research the Constitution of New Zealand thanks to this SCP and/or had the legal knowledge to know that such a document didn't exist! In answer to your question, the Constitution was simply codified non-anomalously. Research indicates there's apparently quite a bit of popular support for the movement and I think the only difference between "our" universe and this universe is that the appropriate bills passed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just want to clarify whether the existence of a Constitution of NZ is supposed to be anomalous in and of itself? Since there currently is currently no single document that comprises the New Zealand constitution and has never been codified. Just thought this was interesting since the debate around whether we should have a codified single document is semi-controversial in the law field at the moment.
Perhaps the date of codification should be moved later into the future for realism?

I like the concept of this, I think it's interesting and like the writing style!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're right; I think that in most headcanons this would be considered pretty lax. I think there's an exception for these guys, mainly because they are Foundation employees, or at least close enough facsimiles to such a degree that it's difficult to pick out the things that would out them as impostors. Obviously, the Foundation wants to know all they can about 73-Waitangi and keeping the future!Foundation's employees happy by, well, treating them as employees is a simple step in this direction.

That being said, having people with knowledge of the future running around uncontained might lead to more paradoxes and bigger headaches for Thad and co., so they keep them cordoned off from staff at large so as to prevent any possible "so who won the Superbowl in your universe, let me make a bet real quick" paradoxes from occurring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like the way the -B instances are handled (sequestered but considered employees) seems really lax for the foundation, but most of my understanding of that kind of stuff is based on several years ago, so maybe things have changed since then.

item.title: the late merle highchurch rolled a 5
